story:
Maya didn’t notice the window across the street until her third month in the city. Her apartment was nothing special—cracked walls, groaning pipes, a fifth-floor climb—but it was hers, and that meant quiet, that meant boxed wine and the hum of traffic for company. One night she leaned against her own window and finally saw it: a matching window, same height, same size, thin curtains glowing around the shape of a man watching her.

She tried to laugh it off. Big city, lots of neighbors. But the figure stayed. Night after night. When she waved, he didn’t—until, slowly, he pressed his palm to the glass. She matched him. Silly. Childish. Intimate. Then his mouth moved, soft beneath the gauze: You can see me? She couldn’t hear him, but she understood. Before she could respond, the curtains fell closed.

He vanished for days. When he returned, closer, she read his name off a notepad pressed to the glass: Adrian. She wrote hers in return. That became their ritual—bright notes, crooked drawings, silent jokes. He told her he was a writer. That he hadn’t spoken to anyone in years. That her attention felt like sunlight. He rarely smiled, but when he did, the room felt warmer. Maya told herself it was nothing. She knew better. She was falling.

Then Mrs. Delgado from downstairs stopped her in the stairwell. “You keep looking at that corner window,” the old woman said, eyes sharp. That building’s been empty for years. Condemned. No one lives there.

Maya’s hands shook as she wrote across the gap: Someone said your building’s empty. Adrian stepped from the shadows, read, and looked afraid. He wrote back: It is.

What are you? she asked the next night, letters bold and uneven. He hesitated, then held up one word: Trapped. He pressed his hand to the glass, lips shaping the same question: You can see me? She nodded. Relief softened his eyes. Then maybe you can free me.

In pieces, he explained. He had lived there decades ago. There had been an accident, a fall down the stairs. He had not left since. People looked past him as if his window were blank. But Maya didn’t. And that mattered. Their attention to each other wasn’t chance; it was a bridge. If they strengthened it, he might cross.

They tested the edge of what they were. His palm against his glass, hers against hers, until warmth bled through where there should have been cold. He whispered without sound, and she felt the words hum inside her chest. Lights flickered when she touched the window. A door creaked open on its own. A shadow moved through her kitchen. Adrian apologized; the bond was bleeding too far. Maya should have been frightened. She was. And still—she stayed.

On a storm-lashed night, the wind howling down the street, their hands met in the window’s reflection. Adrian glowed faintly, like lightning had lit him from within. “I want to see you,” Maya whispered to no one and to him. His mouth shaped it again, reverent: You can see me? “Yes,” she said, and meant it.

Something gave. The glass trembled. Heat. Skin. A second heartbeat. And then Adrian stumbled through, collapsing onto her floor, rain-slick and laughing, alive enough to breathe against her ear. “You pulled me through,” he said, voice raw. Maya cupped his face—no curtain, no glass, just him—and held on as outside the storm raged and, inside, two people reached for the life they’d made between their windows.
